title: Protecting your money
description: Build the safety net before life takes a swing.
summaryPoints: Insurance trades a small premium for protection from giant losses — it never makes you money
Health, auto, renters, life: each type covers one specific kind of disaster
An emergency fund is 3–6 months of expenses in a separate, easy-to-reach account
Real banks never ask for logins or PINs through links — verify through official apps
Protection is a budget line item, not an afterthought

content.title: The level you can't skip
content.body:
You've learned to earn, budget, save, even invest. Here's the plot twist: none of it is safe yet.

One bad day — a crash, an illness, a scam — can erase years of careful progress if nothing is standing guard. That's what financial protection is: the difference between a rough week and years of debt.

This level is about defense: insurance, emergency funds, and scam-proofing. Less flashy than investing. Way more important than it looks.

content.title: Insurance: the group deal
content.body:
Insurance is a group deal: lots of people each pay a small regular fee — a **premium** — into a shared pool. When disaster hits one of them, the pool pays the giant bill.

The big four:
- **Health** — medical bills, so one illness isn't a financial catastrophe
- **Auto** — crash costs (your car AND other people's), usually required by law
- **Renters/Homeowners** — your home and belongings after fire, theft, disasters
- **Life** — money for the people who depend on you if you die
content.imageAlt: Umbrella shielding a house, a car, and a heart

content.prompt: Match the situation to the insurance built for it.

pairs.left: Broke your arm at soccer practice
Laptop stolen from your first apartment
Fender-bender on the drive to school

content.prompt: Insurance is a way to make money over time.
content.explanation: Insurance never earns you money — it stops one giant, unpredictable loss from wiping you out. That protection IS the product.

content.prompt: You pay $40 a month for health insurance and never get sick all year. Did you waste $480?
options: No — you bought protection from a bill that could've been thousands
Yes — you should have invested it instead
No — the company refunds unused premiums
content.explanation: A premium buys certainty: a small known cost instead of a huge unknown one. 'Nothing happened' is the good outcome.

content.title: Your personal backup: the emergency fund
content.body:
Insurance covers the big named disasters. For everything else, there's your **emergency fund** — money YOU set aside, ready the moment life misbehaves.

- **Size:** 3–6 months of living expenses (build toward it — any start counts)
- **Where:** a separate savings account you can reach fast but don't touch daily
- **For:** job loss, car repairs, medical surprises, emergency travel

Without one, surprises land on a credit card and become debt. With one, you just pay — and move on.
content.imageAlt: Umbrella protecting a piggy bank from rain

content.prompt: The classic emergency fund target is ___ to 6 months of living expenses.
content.hint: It's the smaller end of the range.
content.explanation: 3–6 months of expenses covers most job losses and big surprises. Start small — the habit matters more than the starting size.

content.context: You have $500 in your emergency fund. Same week: your car needs a $400 repair, and friends invite you on a $400 weekend trip.
options: Put the trip on a credit card and pay cash for the repair
The trip — the car has lasted this long
The repair — then build the fund back up
Split it: $250 to each and hope
content.explanation: A trip is a want; a working car is a need. This is exactly the moment the fund exists for — use it, skip the trip, refill it. Zero debt involved.

content.prompt: Who handles it: insurance or your emergency fund?

items.text: Hospital bill after a bad fall
You crash into a parked car
Laptop dies the week before finals
Your work hours get cut for a month
Last-minute flight home for a family emergency

content.title: Scam-proofing 101
content.body:
Scammers don't pick locks — they ask you to open the door. Four habits keep it shut:
- **Strong, unique passwords** for every money account (one leak shouldn't open them all)
- **Check your statements** — catch weird charges in days, not months
- **Doubt 'too good to be true'** — prizes, deals, and urgent warnings are bait
- **Shred documents** with personal info before binning them

Recovering from fraud takes months. Preventing it takes minutes.

content.context: An email that looks like your bank says your account is locked and asks you to click a link and log in.
options: Click the link — it looks official
Forward it to friends so they're warned
Reply and ask if it's really the bank
Delete it and contact your bank through its official app or number
content.explanation: Real banks never ask you to log in through an emailed link. Going through the official app means YOU choose who you're talking to.

content.prompt: A 'your account is suspended!' text just landed. Put the smart response in order.
steps: Pause — don't click the link or reply
Open your bank's official app yourself
Check whether anything is actually wrong
content.explanation: Slow is safe. You verify through a door YOU open — never the one the message hands you.

content.title: Protection is a line item
content.body:
Protection isn't a vibe — it's a line in your budget.
- Write your financial goals down (future-you forgets)
- Treat insurance and emergency savings like bills, paid every month
- See big costs coming — school, moving out, a car — and start saving early

A student who saves a little toward college every month borrows far less later. Planned money is cheap; panicked money is expensive.

content.instructions: One word turns this into bad advice. Tap it.
content.sentence: Keep your emergency fund somewhere separate and hard to reach.
content.explanation: Emergencies move fast. The fund lives in a separate account so you don't spend it casually — but you must be able to reach it quickly when it counts.

content.prompt: Your living costs are $1,000 a month. Slide to the smallest emergency fund that hits the 3-month mark.
content.explanation: 3 × $1,000 = $3,000 minimum — and $6,000 reaches the comfy six-month end of the range.

content.title: The Year Nothing Could Shake You
content.intro: Twelve months, four curveballs. Build your defenses before life swings.

steps.situation: New job! It offers health insurance for $40 a month out of your paycheck.

options.text: Enroll — $40 for a giant safety net
options.feedback: One ER visit can cost thousands. $40 a month is the cheap version of that bill.

options.text: Skip it — you never get sick
options.feedback: Nobody plans to get sick. That's the whole point of insurance.

options.text: Decide at the end of the year
options.feedback: Unprotected months are exactly when surprises love to show up.

options.text: Start an emergency fund with it
options.feedback: The first $300 of your 3-month cushion. Future-you just exhaled.
Wants are fine WHEN the safety net exists. It doesn't yet.

steps.situation: A caller says you won a $1,000 gift card — just confirm your card number to claim it.

options.feedback: Real prizes never need your card number. Hanging up costs you nothing.

options.text: Give the number — it's a PRIZE
options.feedback: That 'prize' just funded a shopping spree on your card.

options.text: Ask them to call back later
options.feedback: Scammers love a second chance. Hang up and block.

steps.situation: Your bike — your ride to work — breaks. The fix is $120.

options.text: Pay from the emergency fund, refill it next month
options.feedback: This is exactly the job the fund was hired for. No debt, no drama.

options.text: Put it on a credit card and pay minimums
options.feedback: A $120 problem just signed up to grow interest.

content.outro: Insurance for the big stuff, a fund for the rest, and a healthy distrust of 'free' — that's a year nothing could shake.
